question: What is Angular and how is it different from AngularJS?
answer:
Angular (v2+) is a TypeScript-based, component-driven SPA framework by Google. It ships a full platform: components, dependency injection, routing, forms, HTTP, and RxJS.

AngularJS (v1.x) was JavaScript-based and used controllers, scopes, and two-way digest-cycle binding. Angular is a complete rewrite: component architecture, a hierarchical injector, AOT compilation, and far better performance.
tip: Say "Angular" for 2+ and "AngularJS" only for 1.x — interviewers notice the distinction.

question: Lifecycle hooks — order and what each is for.
answer:
Order: constructor → ngOnChanges → ngOnInit → ngDoCheck → ngAfterContentInit → ngAfterContentChecked → ngAfterViewInit → ngAfterViewChecked → ngOnDestroy.

  • ngOnChanges — runs when an @Input changes (receives SimpleChanges).
  • ngOnInit — one-time init; fetch data here, not in the constructor.
  • ngAfterViewInit — view and @ViewChild refs are ready.
  • ngOnDestroy — cleanup: unsubscribe, clear timers.
tip: The constructor is for dependency injection only; the DOM and inputs aren't ready yet — do real work in ngOnInit.
deep:
The full sequence & how often each runs

HookWhenHow often
constructorclass instantiated (DI only)once
ngOnChangesa bound @Input changesevery input change
ngOnInitafter first ngOnChanges — inputs setonce
ngDoCheckevery change-detection runfrequently
ngAfterContentInitprojected content (ng-content) readyonce
ngAfterContentCheckedafter projected content checkedfrequently
ngAfterViewInitcomponent's view + child views readyonce
ngAfterViewCheckedafter the view is checkedfrequently
ngOnDestroyjust before the component is destroyedonce

Why not just use the constructor?

The constructor runs before Angular has wired up inputs or rendered the view. At that point @Input values are still undefined and @ViewChild refs don't exist. So the constructor is only for dependency injection; put initialization logic (data fetching, setup that needs inputs) in ngOnInit.

ngAfterViewInit & @ViewChild timing

ngAfterViewInit fires once, after the component's template (and its children's views) are rendered in the DOM. This is the earliest point a @ViewChild / @ViewChildren reference is reliably available — e.g. to init a chart or measure an element.

If a @ViewChild is inside an *ngIf/@if, it resolves later — use { static: false } (the default) and read it in ngAfterViewInit, not ngOnInit.

ngOnDestroy — the cleanup hook

Runs once, right before Angular destroys the component. It's crucial for preventing memory leaks. Use it to:

  • Unsubscribe from long-lived RxJS streams (Subjects, valueChanges, intervals).
  • Detach manual event listeners you added.
  • Clear setTimeout/setInterval timers.
private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();

ngOnInit() {
  this.data$.pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$)).subscribe(...);
}
ngOnDestroy() {
  this.destroy$.next();
  this.destroy$.complete();
}

(HTTP calls complete on their own, so they don't strictly need cleanup — but streams that never complete do.)

Common pitfalls

  • Doing DOM work in ngOnInit — the view isn't ready; use ngAfterViewInit.
  • Heavy logic in ngDoCheck/…Checked hooks — they run on every cycle and will tank performance.
  • Forgetting ngOnDestroy cleanup — the #1 source of Angular memory leaks.
  • Updating a value in ngAfterViewInit that's shown in the same view → ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenChecked error (defer with a microtask or move to ngOnInit).

How to say it in an interview

“The constructor is only for DI. I fetch data in ngOnInit once inputs are set, react to input changes in ngOnChanges, touch the rendered DOM / @ViewChild in ngAfterViewInit, and always clean up subscriptions and timers in ngOnDestroy to avoid leaks.”

question: What are the types of data binding?
answer:
• Interpolation — {{ value }} (component → view).
  • Property binding — [src]="url" (component → view).
  • Event binding — (click)="fn()" (view → component).
  • Two-way binding — [(ngModel)]="name" (both directions; the "banana in a box").
tip: Two-way binding is just property + event binding combined under the hood.
deep:
The four bindings and their direction

BindingSyntaxDirection
Interpolation{{ value }}class → view
Property[prop]="value"class → view
Event(event)="fn($event)"view → class
Two-way[(ngModel)]="value"both

Interpolation vs property binding

Interpolation is a string shortcut — {{ x }} is coerced to text. Property binding sets the actual DOM property with the evaluated type, so use it whenever the value isn't a string:

<!-- string attribute -->
<img src="{{ url }}">
<!-- real property, keeps type -->
<img [src]="url">
<input [disabled]="isLocked">  <!-- boolean, not the string "false" -->

A common bug: disabled="{{ false }}" renders disabled="false" which the DOM still treats as disabled. [disabled]="false" works correctly.

How two-way binding really works

The "banana in a box" [()] is pure syntax sugar for a property binding + an event binding. Angular expands:

<input [(ngModel)]="name">
<!-- is exactly -->
<input [ngModel]="name" (ngModelChange)="name = $event">

Any input paired with an output named <input>Change becomes two-way bindable — that's how you build your own (see the custom two-way binding question).

Attribute vs property binding

Most bindings target DOM properties. A few things only exist as HTML attributes (ARIA, colspan, SVG) — for those use [attr.aria-label]="...". Similar helpers: [class.active], [style.width.px].

How to say it in an interview

“There are four: interpolation and property binding push data to the view, event binding sends events back, and two-way binding combines a property and an event binding — it’s literally [x] plus (xChange) under the hood.”

question: What are the types of directives?
answer:
• Component — a directive with a template (the most common).
  • Structural — change DOM layout: *ngIf, *ngFor, *ngSwitch (v17+: @if, @for).
  • Attribute — change appearance/behaviour: ngClass, ngStyle, or your own.
tip: The leading * on structural directives is sugar for an <ng-template> wrapper.

question: How do components communicate with each other?
answer:
• Parent → child: @Input().
  • Child → parent: @Output() with an EventEmitter.
  • Unrelated components: a shared service with a BehaviorSubject (or a signal / NgRx store).
  • Parent accessing child: @ViewChild.
tip: For siblings/unrelated components, prefer a shared service over chaining inputs and outputs.
code:
@Output() saved = new EventEmitter<Item>();
onSave() { this.saved.emit(this.item); }

question: Explain Dependency Injection and providedIn:'root'.
answer:
Angular has a hierarchical injector. You declare a dependency in a constructor and Angular supplies the instance. A service is a class holding shared logic/state, injected wherever needed.

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) registers the service once as an app-wide singleton and makes it tree-shakable. Providing it at component level instead gives each component its own instance.
tip: Tree-shakable providers (providedIn) are preferred over listing services in NgModule providers.
code:
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class UserService {}

question: Template-driven vs Reactive forms — which and why?
answer:
Template-driven — logic lives in the template via ngModel; quick, good for simple forms.

Reactive — the form model is defined in the TS class (FormGroup, FormControl, FormBuilder); explicit, testable, and better for complex or dynamic validation.
tip: For anything non-trivial, reach for Reactive forms — they're easier to unit test.
deep:
Side-by-side

Template-drivenReactive
Model lives inthe template (ngModel)the TS class (FormGroup)
ModuleFormsModuleReactiveFormsModule
Data flowasync (two-way)synchronous & explicit
Validationdirectives in templatefunctions in class
Dynamic controlshardeasy (FormArray)
Unit testingneeds the DOMtest the model directly
Best forsimple, small formscomplex / dynamic forms

Template-driven — quick and declarative

<form #f="ngForm" (ngSubmit)="save(f.value)">
  <input name="email" ngModel required email>
</form>

Angular builds the FormGroup for you behind the scenes, one FormControl per ngModel. Validation is added as attribute directives.

Reactive — explicit and testable

form = this.fb.group({
  email: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.email]],
  password: ['', Validators.minLength(8)],
});

save() {
  if (this.form.invalid) return;
  this.api.login(this.form.value);
}

You hold the source of truth. You can valueChanges.pipe(debounceTime(300)), add/remove controls at runtime, and unit-test without rendering anything.

Which and why (the answer they want)

“I default to Reactive forms for anything beyond a trivial form: the model is in TypeScript so it’s type-safe, testable, and supports dynamic controls and reactive validation. I only use template-driven for a quick, small form like a single search box.”

Common pitfalls

  • Mixing ngModel with a reactive formControlName on the same field — pick one.
  • Forgetting to import the right module (ReactiveFormsModule vs FormsModule).
  • Reading form.value and expecting disabled controls — they’re excluded; use getRawValue().

question: Explain routing: lazy loading, guards, and resolvers.
answer:
• Lazy loading — load a feature only when visited via loadChildren / loadComponent. Smaller initial bundle, faster first paint.
  • Guards — CanActivate (auth), CanDeactivate (unsaved-changes prompt), CanMatch.
  • Resolver — pre-fetch data before the route activates so the component opens with data ready.
tip: Lazy loading is the single biggest lever for initial load performance in large apps.
code:
{ path: 'admin', loadComponent: () =>
  import('./admin.component').then(m => m.AdminComponent) }
deep:
Routing in Angular

Angular Router enables navigation between different views without reloading the page. A route maps a URL to a component. In large applications, routing is commonly combined with lazy loading, route guards, and resolvers to improve performance, security, and user experience.

1. Lazy Loading

Lazy loading means a feature is downloaded only when the user navigates to it instead of being included in the initial bundle. This reduces the application's initial download size and improves load time.

const routes: Routes = [
  {
    path: 'admin',
    loadChildren: () =>
      import('./admin/admin.routes').then(m => m.ADMIN_ROUTES)
  }
];

Example: An e-commerce application has Customer, Admin, Reports, and Analytics modules. Most users never visit Admin, so downloading it only when required significantly improves startup performance.

2. Route Guards

Guards decide whether navigation should be allowed. They are commonly used for authentication, authorization, preventing data loss, and conditional routing.

  • CanActivate – Prevents access to a route if the user is not authorized.
  • CanDeactivate – Prevents leaving a page with unsaved changes.
  • CanMatch – Determines whether a route should match before loading it.
  • CanActivateChild – Protects child routes.
export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = () => {
  return inject(AuthService).isLoggedIn();
};

{
  path: 'admin',
  canActivate: [authGuard],
  loadComponent: () => import('./admin.component')
    .then(m => m.AdminComponent)
}

Real-world example: In a banking application, if the user is not logged in and tries to open /accounts, the guard redirects them to the login page.

3. Resolver

A resolver fetches required data before the component is created. This ensures the page opens with data already available instead of showing loading spinners inside the component.

export const userResolver: ResolveFn<User> = (route) => {
  return inject(UserService).getUser(route.params['id']);
};

{
  path: 'users/:id',
  component: UserComponent,
  resolve: {
    user: userResolver
  }
}
export class UserComponent {
  user = inject(ActivatedRoute).snapshot.data['user'];
}

Real-world example: When opening a customer profile, the customer's details are loaded before the page is displayed, avoiding an empty screen followed by a loading spinner.

Putting them together

{
  path: 'admin',
  canActivate: [authGuard],
  resolve: {
    settings: settingsResolver
  },
  loadComponent: () =>
    import('./admin.component').then(m => m.AdminComponent)
}

Navigation flow:

  1. User navigates to /admin.
  2. Angular lazy-loads the Admin feature.
  3. The authentication guard checks whether the user is logged in.
  4. If allowed, the resolver fetches required settings or user data.
  5. Finally, the Admin component is displayed with all required data.

Interview Tip

Remember the sequence: Lazy Loading → Guard → Resolver → Component. Explain that lazy loading improves performance, guards protect routes, and resolvers ensure data is ready before rendering. This is the architecture commonly used in enterprise Angular applications.

question: What are HTTP interceptors used for?
answer: HTTP Interceptors are middleware that intercept every outgoing HTTP request and incoming HTTP response in an Angular application. They allow you to add, modify, or handle requests and responses globally without changing individual service methods. Common uses include attaching JWT tokens, adding custom headers, logging, displaying global loaders, handling errors, retrying failed requests, and refreshing expired access tokens.
tip: Think of an interceptor as Angular's middleware. Every request passes through it before reaching the server, and every response passes through it before reaching your application.
deep:
What is an HTTP Interceptor?

An HTTP Interceptor is a service that sits between your Angular application and the backend server. Every outgoing request and incoming response automatically passes through the interceptor.

Component
      ↓
UserService
      ↓
HttpClient
      ↓
HTTP Interceptor
      ↓
Backend API
      ↑
HTTP Response
      ↑
HTTP Interceptor
      ↑
Component

This allows common logic to be implemented once instead of repeating it in every API call.

Common Uses

  • Attach JWT or OAuth access tokens.
  • Add common HTTP headers.
  • Display a global loading spinner.
  • Log requests and responses.
  • Handle HTTP errors globally.
  • Refresh expired access tokens.
  • Retry failed requests.
  • Measure request duration.

Adding Authentication Token

intercept(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler) {
  const token = localStorage.getItem('token');

  const authReq = req.clone({
    setHeaders: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`
    }
  });

  return next.handle(authReq);
}

Since HttpRequest is immutable, you must call clone() before modifying it.

Global Error Handling

return next.handle(req).pipe(
  catchError((error: HttpErrorResponse) => {
    if (error.status === 401) {
      console.log('Unauthorized');
    }

    if (error.status === 500) {
      console.log('Server Error');
    }

    return throwError(() => error);
  })
);

Instead of writing catchError() in every service, one interceptor can handle errors for the entire application.

Loading Spinner

intercept(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler) {
  this.loader.show();

  return next.handle(req).pipe(
    finalize(() => this.loader.hide())
  );
}

The loader appears before the request and disappears automatically when the request finishes.

Refresh Token Flow

User Request
      ↓
Access Token Expired
      ↓
401 Unauthorized
      ↓
Interceptor
      ↓
Refresh Token API
      ↓
New Access Token
      ↓
Retry Original Request

This is one of the most common enterprise use cases for HTTP Interceptors.

Functional Interceptors (Angular 15+)

Modern Angular supports functional interceptors using HttpInterceptorFn.

export const authInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  const token = localStorage.getItem('token');

  return next(
    req.clone({
      setHeaders: {
        Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`
      }
    })
  );
};

Functional interceptors are tree-shakable and are the recommended approach in standalone Angular applications.

Registering an Interceptor

Standalone Angular (Recommended)

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, {
  providers: [
    provideHttpClient(
      withInterceptors([authInterceptor])
    )
  ]
});

NgModule

providers: [
  {
    provide: HTTP_INTERCEPTORS,
    useClass: AuthInterceptor,
    multi: true
  }
]

Execution Order

If multiple interceptors are registered, requests flow in the order they are provided, while responses flow back in the reverse order.

Request
Auth Interceptor
      ↓
Loader Interceptor
      ↓
Logging Interceptor
      ↓
Backend
      ↑
Logging
      ↑
Loader
      ↑
Auth

This ordering is important when combining authentication, logging, and loading indicators.

Real-world Enterprise Example

In most enterprise Angular applications, a request follows this pipeline:

  1. Attach JWT token.
  2. Show loading spinner.
  3. Send request.
  4. Handle errors globally.
  5. If 401 occurs, refresh the token.
  6. Retry the original request.
  7. Hide the loader.

This keeps services focused only on business logic while cross-cutting concerns are handled centrally.

Interview Tip

Interviewers commonly ask 'Why use an interceptor instead of adding headers inside every service?' The answer is that interceptors centralize common logic, reduce code duplication, and make maintenance easier. Also mention that HttpRequest objects are immutable, so you must always use clone() before modifying a request.

Interview Answer

"HTTP Interceptors act as middleware for Angular's HttpClient. Every request and response passes through them, allowing developers to implement cross-cutting concerns such as authentication, adding headers, logging, global loading indicators, error handling, token refresh, and retry logic in one place. Requests are immutable, so they must be cloned before modification, and interceptors execute in the order they are registered."

question: Explain content projection (ng-content) and ViewChild/ContentChild.
answer:
ng-content projects markup that a parent passes into a child (like slots) — key for reusable UI such as cards and modals.

@ViewChild references an element/component in this template; @ContentChild references projected content. They're ready in ngAfterViewInit and ngAfterContentInit respectively.
tip: Use multi-slot projection with select: <ng-content select="[header]">.
deep:
Content projection = slots

ng-content lets a parent pass markup into a child, so the child owns the shell and the parent owns the content. This is how you build reusable <app-card>, modals, and layout wrappers.

<!-- card.component.html -->
<div class="card">
  <header><ng-content select="[card-title]"></ng-content></header>
  <div class="body"><ng-content></ng-content></div>
</div>

<!-- usage -->
<app-card>
  <h2 card-title>Title</h2>
  <p>Any body markup here.</p>
</app-card>

The first selected slot catches matching elements; the plain <ng-content> catches everything else.

ViewChild vs ContentChild — the key difference

@ViewChild@ContentChild
Querieselements in this component's own templateelements projected in via ng-content
Ready inngAfterViewInitngAfterContentInit

Mnemonic: View = what you wrote in your template; Content = what someone else handed you through the slot.

@ViewChild('input') input!: ElementRef;      // in my template
@ContentChild(TabComponent) tab!: TabComponent; // projected in

ngAfterViewInit()    { this.input.nativeElement.focus(); }
ngAfterContentInit() { /* this.tab is available here */ }

Why the timing matters

Reading a @ViewChild in ngOnInit often returns undefined because the view isn't rendered yet — read it in ngAfterViewInit. Same for @ContentChild and ngAfterContentInit. (Signal queries in v17+ remove much of this timing pain.)

How to say it in an interview

“ng-content projects a parent’s markup into a reusable child, like slots. @ViewChild grabs something from my own template and is ready in ngAfterViewInit; @ContentChild grabs projected content and is ready in ngAfterContentInit.”

question: How does change detection work? Default vs OnPush.
answer:
zone.js patches async APIs and tells Angular to re-check the component tree after events. The Default strategy checks every component each cycle.

OnPush only re-checks a component when: an @Input reference changes, an event fires inside it, or an async pipe emits. Combined with immutable data it drastically cuts re-renders. (Signals push toward zone-less change detection.)
tip: OnPush + immutable data is the highest-impact perf change you can describe in an interview.
code: @Component({ changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush })
deep:
What actually triggers change detection

zone.js monkey-patches async browser APIs — setTimeout, addEventListener, Promise, XHR/fetch. When any of them fires, Zone tells Angular “something might have changed,” and Angular runs change detection over the component tree, top to bottom.

Each pass re-evaluates every template binding and compares the new value to the previous one; if it differs, the DOM is updated.

Default vs OnPush

DefaultOnPush
When checkedevery component, every cycleonly when a trigger applies
Triggersany async event anywhere@Input reference change, event inside the component, async pipe emit, or manual markForCheck()
Costhighermuch lower in big trees

Why OnPush needs immutable data

OnPush compares @Input by reference. If you mutate an object/array in place, the reference is unchanged and the view won't update:

// ❌ mutation — same reference, OnPush won't see it
this.items.push(newItem);

// ✅ new reference — OnPush updates
this.items = [...this.items, newItem];

Forcing a check when you must

  • ChangeDetectorRef.markForCheck() — mark this component + ancestors to be checked next cycle (the OnPush-friendly one).
  • detectChanges() — run detection synchronously now, on this subtree.
  • detach() / reattach() — take a component out of the tree entirely for manual control.

Where Signals fit

Signals give Angular fine-grained reactivity — it knows exactly which template expressions depend on which signal, so it can update just those without walking the whole tree. This is the path to zoneless apps (drop zone.js entirely), which the framework is moving toward.

How to say it in an interview

“zone.js notifies Angular after async events and Default checks the whole tree. I switch hot components to OnPush, which only re-checks on input-reference changes, internal events, or async-pipe emissions — so I keep data immutable. Signals push this further toward zoneless, per-binding updates.”

question: List concrete performance optimisations in Angular.
answer:
• ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush + immutable data.
  • trackBy in *ngFor (or track in @for) to reuse DOM nodes.
  • Lazy load feature routes; add a preloading strategy for the rest.
  • Pure pipes instead of method calls in templates.
  • Virtual scrolling (cdk-virtual-scroll) for long lists.
  • async pipe to avoid manual-subscription leaks.
tip: Never call a method in a template binding — it runs on every change-detection cycle.

question: Pure vs impure pipes — and what is a pipe?
answer:
A pipe transforms a value in the template ({{ date | date:'short' }}).

Pure (default) — recomputes only when the input reference changes; cheap. Impure — runs every change-detection cycle (e.g. async, or a filter over a mutating array); powerful but costly, use sparingly.
tip: The async pipe is impure by necessity — it must react to new emissions.
code: @Pipe({ name: 'myFilter', pure: false })
deep:
What a pipe is

A pipe is a small class that transforms a value for display in a template, without changing the underlying data. You chain them with | and pass arguments after :.

{{ price | currency:'INR' }}
{{ date | date:'mediumDate' }}
{{ name | uppercase }}
{{ items | slice:0:5 }}

Pure vs impure

Pure (default)Impure
Runs wheninput reference changesevery change-detection cycle
Costcheapexpensive
Examplesdate, currency, uppercaseasync, a live filter/sort

The mutation trap

A pure pipe won't re-run if you mutate its input in place, because the reference didn't change:

this.items.push(x);       // ❌ pure filter pipe won't re-run
this.items = [...this.items, x]; // ✅ new reference, re-runs

Making the pipe impure (pure: false) fixes display but runs it on every cycle — a performance risk on large lists.

Don't filter/sort with pipes on big lists

The Angular team explicitly advises against filter/orderBy pipes (that's why they were removed from Angular). Prefer computing the filtered list in the component (or a computed() signal) so it only recalculates when inputs actually change.

@Pipe({ name: 'initials', standalone: true })
export class InitialsPipe implements PipeTransform {
  transform(name: string): string {
    return name.split(' ').map(w => w[0]).join('');
  }
}

How to say it in an interview

“A pipe transforms a value for display. Pure pipes — the default — only recompute when the input reference changes, so they’re cheap; impure pipes run every cycle. The async pipe is impure because it has to react to new emissions. I avoid filtering big lists in a pipe and do it in the component or a computed signal.”

question: What are Signals? How do they differ from RxJS?
answer:
Signals (v16+) are a fine-grained reactivity primitive: a wrapper around a value that notifies consumers when it changes, enabling precise, zone-less change detection.

vs RxJS: signals are synchronous, always hold a current value, and are ideal for local component state. RxJS is for streams / async events over time. They interoperate via toSignal / toObservable.
tip: Mention Signals even for older-Angular roles — it signals you keep up with the framework.
deep:
The three primitives

  • signal(v) — a writable box holding a value; read with count(), write with .set() / .update().
  • computed(fn) — a derived, read-only signal that recalculates only when a signal it reads changes (memoized).
  • effect(fn) — runs a side effect whenever any signal it reads changes (logging, syncing to storage).
count = signal(0);
double = computed(() => this.count() * 2);

this.count.set(5);      // double() is now 10
this.count.update(n => n + 1);

Signals vs RxJS

SignalObservable (RxJS)
Has a current valuealways (pull)no — values arrive over time (push)
Sync/asyncsynchronouscan be async
Best forlocal/UI stateevents, streams, HTTP, debounce
Operatorsfew (computed/effect)hundreds (map, switchMap…)
Subscriptionnone to managemust subscribe/unsubscribe

The mental model

A signal answers “what is the value right now?”. An observable answers “notify me each time a new value happens over time.” Use signals for state you render; use RxJS for asynchronous event pipelines.

They interoperate

// stream → signal (for templates)
user = toSignal(this.userService.user$, { initialValue: null });

// signal → stream (to use RxJS operators)
query$ = toObservable(this.query).pipe(debounceTime(300));

Why they matter

Signals enable fine-grained, zone-less change detection: Angular updates exactly the bindings that read a changed signal instead of re-checking the whole tree. That's the framework's direction going forward.

How to say it in an interview

“Signals are a synchronous reactivity primitive that always holds a current value — perfect for local component state and fine-grained change detection. RxJS is for asynchronous streams of values over time. They’re complementary, and toSignal/toObservable bridge them.”

question: What are standalone components and the new control flow?
answer:
Standalone components (v14+, default in v17) drop NgModule — a component declares its own imports. Simpler, less boilerplate.

Built-in control flow (v17): @if, @for (requires track), and @switch — replaces *ngIf/*ngFor, and is faster.
tip: @for requires a track expression — it's not optional like trackBy was.

question: What is View Encapsulation?
answer:
Controls how a component's styles are scoped:

  • Emulated (default) — Angular adds attribute selectors so styles don't leak; no real Shadow DOM.
  • ShadowDom — uses native Shadow DOM for true isolation.
  • None — styles are global.
tip: Use ::ng-deep sparingly to pierce encapsulation — it's deprecated and leaks globally.
code: @Component({ encapsulation: ViewEncapsulation.Emulated })
deep:
The problem it solves

Normally CSS is global — a .title rule in one component would style every .title on the page. View Encapsulation scopes a component's styles so they only apply to that component's own template.

The three modes

ModeHowIsolation
Emulated (default)Angular rewrites selectors and adds a unique attribute (_ngcontent-xyz) to each elementscoped, but no real DOM boundary
ShadowDomrenders the component in a native Shadow DOM treetrue, browser-enforced isolation
Nonestyles are injected globally, untouchednone — leaks everywhere

What Emulated actually generates

/* you write */
h1 { color: red; }

/* Angular emits */
h1[_ngcontent-abc] { color: red; }
<h1 _ngcontent-abc>...</h1>

So the rule can only match elements Angular tagged for this component — that's the "emulation" of scoping, done with plain attribute selectors (no Shadow DOM needed, works everywhere).

Piercing encapsulation

  • :host — style the component's own host element.
  • :host-context(.dark) — style based on an ancestor's class.
  • ::ng-deep — force a style into child components. Deprecated and effectively global — prefer restructuring or a shared style.

ShadowDom caveats

Real isolation also means global styles don't reach in, and events like focus retarget at the boundary. Great for true web-component reuse; overkill for a normal app.

How to say it in an interview

“By default Angular uses Emulated encapsulation — it adds a unique attribute to each element and rewrites my selectors so styles stay scoped without real Shadow DOM. ShadowDom gives true browser isolation, and None makes styles global. I avoid ::ng-deep since it’s deprecated and leaks.”

question: AOT vs JIT compilation.
answer:
AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compiles templates at build time — smaller bundles, faster rendering, template errors caught early, safer. It's the default for production.

JIT (Just-in-Time) compiles in the browser at runtime — historically used in development.
tip: Modern Angular (Ivy) uses AOT everywhere by default.
deep:
What is Angular Compilation?

Angular templates (.html) cannot be executed directly by the browser. Angular first compiles templates into efficient JavaScript instructions that create and update the DOM. This compilation can happen either before the application is deployed (AOT) or inside the browser at runtime (JIT).

1. JIT (Just-in-Time) Compilation

With JIT, Angular downloads both the application code and the Angular compiler. When the application starts, the browser compiles templates into executable JavaScript.

Developer writes:
Component + Template

Browser
    ↓
Angular Compiler
    ↓
Compiled JavaScript
    ↓
Render UI

Advantages:

  • Faster development builds.
  • Ideal during development because changes compile quickly.

Disadvantages:

  • Larger bundle because the Angular compiler is shipped to the browser.
  • Slower application startup.
  • Template errors are detected only when the application runs.

2. AOT (Ahead-of-Time) Compilation

With AOT, Angular compiles templates during the build process. The browser receives only optimized JavaScript and does not need the Angular compiler.

Developer writes:
Component + Template

Angular Build
    ↓
Angular Compiler
    ↓
Optimized JavaScript
    ↓
Deploy
    ↓
Browser renders immediately

Advantages:

  • Smaller bundle size.
  • Faster initial rendering.
  • Template errors are caught during build.
  • Improved security because templates are already compiled.

Disadvantages:

  • Slightly longer build time.

AOT vs JIT

FeatureAOTJIT
CompilationBuild TimeRuntime
Compiler in Bundle❌ No✅ Yes
Initial LoadFastSlower
Bundle SizeSmallerLarger
Template ErrorsBuild TimeRuntime
Used InProductionMainly Development (historically)

What is Ivy?

Ivy is Angular's rendering and compilation engine introduced in Angular 9. It replaced the older View Engine and significantly improved compilation speed, tree-shaking, debugging, and bundle size.

Unlike the old compiler, Ivy converts every component into highly optimized JavaScript instructions.

@Component({
  selector: 'app-user',
  template: '<h1>{{name}}</h1>'
})
export class UserComponent {}

Ivy internally generates optimized instructions similar to:

ɵɵelementStart(...)
ɵɵtext(...)
ɵɵelementEnd(...)

These low-level instructions are much smaller and faster than the metadata generated by the old View Engine.

How Ivy changed AOT

Before Angular 9, developers often used JIT during development and AOT for production. With Ivy, AOT became much faster, so modern Angular uses AOT by default for both development and production builds.

Ivy also enables:

  • Faster incremental builds.
  • Better tree shaking.
  • Smaller bundles.
  • Improved debugging.
  • Standalone Components.
  • Simpler library compilation.

Real-world Example

Imagine an enterprise Angular application with hundreds of components.

  • Using JIT, the browser downloads the Angular compiler and compiles every template when the application starts.
  • Using AOT + Ivy, all templates are already compiled during the build, so the browser immediately renders the application with a smaller bundle and faster startup.

Interview Answer

"AOT compiles Angular templates during the build, producing smaller bundles, faster startup, and compile-time template validation. JIT compiles templates in the browser at runtime and is mainly useful for development. Since Angular 9, Ivy became the default rendering engine, making AOT much faster and enabling Angular to use AOT by default while also improving tree shaking, build performance, and bundle size."

question: What does the static flag on @ViewChild do?
answer:
@ViewChild(ref, { static: true }) resolves the query before change detection, so it's available in ngOnInit — use only for elements that are always present.

static: false (default) resolves after the view initialises, available in ngAfterViewInit — required if the element is inside an *ngIf/*ngFor.
tip: If a ViewChild is undefined, it's almost always a static/timing issue.
code: @ViewChild('chart', { static: false }) chart!: ElementRef;

question: What are @HostListener and @HostBinding?
answer:
Used in directives to interact with the host element without touching the DOM directly.

  • @HostListener — subscribe to a host event (@HostListener('click')).
  • @HostBinding — bind a host property/class/style (@HostBinding('class.active')).
tip: Prefer these over Renderer2 for simple host interactions — they're declarative.
code:
@HostBinding('class.open') isOpen = false;
@HostListener('click') toggle() { this.isOpen = !this.isOpen; }

question: Difference between ng-template, ng-container, and ng-content?
answer:
• ng-template — a template block that is not rendered until referenced (e.g. by a structural directive or ngTemplateOutlet).
  • ng-container — a logical grouping wrapper that adds no element to the DOM (great for applying *ngIf without an extra div).
  • ng-content — a projection slot for parent-supplied markup.
tip: Use ng-container to combine structural directives without wrapper divs.

question: Why is the async pipe preferred over manual subscription?
answer:
The async pipe subscribes to an Observable/Promise, renders the latest value, and automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed — eliminating a whole class of memory leaks.

It also plays well with OnPush: an emission marks the component for check.
tip: Use a single async pipe with *ngIf ... as to avoid multiple subscriptions to the same stream.
code: {{ user.name }}

question: Why use Renderer2 instead of direct DOM access?
answer:
Renderer2 abstracts DOM manipulation so your code works in environments without a real DOM (server-side rendering, web workers) and keeps Angular's security model intact.

Avoid document / nativeElement.innerHTML directly — they break SSR and open XSS risks.
tip: Mention SSR (Angular Universal) as the main reason to avoid direct DOM access.
code:
constructor(private r: Renderer2, private el: ElementRef) {}
ngOnInit() { this.r.addClass(this.el.nativeElement, 'active'); }
deep:
What is Renderer2?

Renderer2 is Angular's abstraction layer for manipulating the DOM. Instead of interacting directly with browser APIs like document or ElementRef.nativeElement, Angular provides Renderer2 so the framework can safely manage DOM updates across different rendering environments.

Why not manipulate the DOM directly?

Using browser APIs such as document.querySelector(), innerHTML, or style tightly couples your code to the browser. This causes problems when Angular runs in environments that don't have a real DOM, such as Server-Side Rendering (Angular Universal) or future rendering platforms.

// ❌ Avoid
const el = document.getElementById('title');
el.style.color = 'red';

// ❌ Avoid
this.elementRef.nativeElement.innerHTML = '<script>alert(1)</script>';

Direct DOM manipulation can also introduce Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities if untrusted HTML is inserted into the page.

Using Renderer2

Renderer2 provides platform-independent methods for common DOM operations.

constructor(
  private renderer: Renderer2,
  private elementRef: ElementRef
) {}

ngOnInit() {
  this.renderer.addClass(this.elementRef.nativeElement, 'active');
}

Common Renderer2 Methods

MethodPurpose
createElement()Create a DOM element
appendChild()Append a child element
removeChild()Remove an element
setAttribute()Add or update attributes
removeAttribute()Remove attributes
setStyle()Apply CSS styles
removeStyle()Remove CSS styles
addClass()Add CSS class
removeClass()Remove CSS class
listen()Register event listeners
setProperty()Update DOM properties

Example: Dynamically Creating an Element

const button = this.renderer.createElement('button');
this.renderer.setProperty(button, 'textContent', 'Save');
this.renderer.addClass(button, 'btn-primary');
this.renderer.appendChild(this.elementRef.nativeElement, button);

Example: Listening to Events

this.renderer.listen(
  this.elementRef.nativeElement,
  'click',
  () => console.log('Clicked')
);

Renderer2 vs ElementRef

Renderer2ElementRef
Platform independentDirect browser DOM access
SaferCan bypass Angular protections
Works with SSRMay fail during SSR
Recommended for DOM changesUse only when absolutely necessary

Real-world Example

Suppose you're building a reusable Tooltip Directive.

  1. User hovers over an element.
  2. The directive dynamically creates a tooltip.
  3. The tooltip is appended to the DOM.
  4. Styles are applied.
  5. When the mouse leaves, the tooltip is removed.

All of these operations should use Renderer2 instead of direct DOM APIs.

const tooltip = this.renderer.createElement('div');
this.renderer.setProperty(tooltip, 'textContent', 'Customer Details');
this.renderer.addClass(tooltip, 'tooltip');
this.renderer.appendChild(document.body, tooltip);

What about Angular Bindings?

In most cases, you should not manipulate the DOM at all. Angular's template syntax already covers common scenarios.

  • Use [class.active] instead of addClass().
  • Use [style.color] instead of setStyle().
  • Use *ngIf instead of manually creating or removing elements.

Use Renderer2 only when you're writing directives or libraries that genuinely need low-level DOM manipulation.

Interview Tip

If asked 'Why Renderer2 instead of ElementRef?', don't answer only 'because it's safer.' Mention all three reasons: platform independence (SSR), security (avoids XSS), and Angular's rendering abstraction. Those are the key points interviewers expect.

Interview Answer

"Renderer2 is Angular's platform-independent API for DOM manipulation. Instead of accessing the DOM directly using document or ElementRef, Renderer2 works safely across browsers and Server-Side Rendering, integrates with Angular's rendering engine, and reduces security risks such as XSS. In practice, I use Angular bindings whenever possible, and only use Renderer2 when building directives or performing dynamic DOM manipulation."

question: What is an NgModule and what are its main metadata fields?
answer:
An @NgModule groups related code into a cohesive block. Key fields:

  • declarations — components, directives, pipes owned by this module.
  • imports — other modules whose exports this module needs.
  • exports — what this module makes available to importers.
  • providers — services (module-level DI).
  • bootstrap — the root component (AppModule only).
tip: Modern Angular is moving to standalone components — mention NgModules are no longer required in v17+.

question: What does ChangeDetectorRef do (markForCheck, detectChanges, detach)?
answer:
ChangeDetectorRef gives you manual control over change detection, mainly with OnPush:

  • markForCheck() — marks this component and ancestors to be checked in the next cycle (async data updated outside Angular).
  • detectChanges() — runs change detection on this component subtree immediately.
  • detach() / reattach() — remove/add the component from the CD tree for extreme perf tuning.
tip: With OnPush, if a value changes but the view doesn't update, you usually need markForCheck().
code:
constructor(private cdr: ChangeDetectorRef) {}
update() { this.data = next; this.cdr.markForCheck(); }

question: What is an InjectionToken and why use one?
answer: Interfaces and primitives disappear at runtime, so they can't be DI keys. An InjectionToken is a unique, type-safe token used to inject non-class values — config objects, feature flags, strings, or interface-typed values.
tip: Use InjectionToken for app config so it's mockable in tests and typed.

question: Explain useClass, useValue, useExisting, useFactory.
answer:
• useClass — provide a class (can swap the implementation, e.g. a mock).
  • useValue — provide a ready value/object.
  • useExisting — alias one token to another (share a single instance).
  • useFactory — build the value with a function (with deps) for conditional logic.

Add multi: true to contribute several values under one token (e.g. HTTP interceptors).
tip: useClass is how you swap a real service for a mock in tests.
code:
{ provide: Logger, useClass: ConsoleLogger }
{ provide: HTTP_INTERCEPTORS, useClass: AuthInterceptor, multi: true }

question: How do you create a custom pipe?
answer: Implement PipeTransform and decorate with @Pipe. The transform method receives the value plus any arguments and returns the transformed result. Keep pipes pure and side-effect free.
tip: Prefer a pure pipe over a component method for template transforms — it only recomputes when inputs change.

question: How do you build a custom attribute directive?
answer: Decorate a class with @Directive, inject ElementRef (and optionally Renderer2), and react to inputs/host events. Attribute directives change appearance or behaviour without a template.
tip: Use @HostListener/@HostBinding inside directives instead of touching the DOM directly.

question: How do custom structural directives work (TemplateRef + ViewContainerRef)?
answer: A structural directive (the * syntax) injects TemplateRef (the template to render) and ViewContainerRef (where to render it). You conditionally create or clear the embedded view — exactly how *ngIf works internally.
tip: The leading * desugars to an <ng-template> wrapper the directive controls.

question: Input setter vs ngOnChanges — when to react to input changes?
answer: Both let you react when an @Input updates. A setter is concise and per-property (transform/validate one input). ngOnChanges receives all changed inputs together in one SimpleChanges object — better when logic depends on several inputs at once.
tip: Use a setter for one input, ngOnChanges when multiple inputs must be considered together.

question: How do you make a custom two-way bindable property?
answer: Two-way binding [(x)] is sugar for an @Input() x plus an @Output() xChange that follows the <prop>Change naming convention. In v17.2+ a single model() signal does both.
tip: The naming convention (valueChange) is what enables the banana-in-a-box on your own component.

question: What is a FormArray and when do you use it?
answer: FormArray manages a dynamic, indexed list of controls/groups — add/remove fields at runtime (e.g. a variable list of phone numbers or line items). Iterate controls in the template and push/removeAt in code.
tip: FormArray is the answer to 'how would you build a form with a dynamic number of fields'.
code:
phones = this.fb.array([this.fb.control('')]);
addPhone() { this.phones.push(this.fb.control('')); }

question: How do you write sync and async custom validators?
answer: A sync validator is a function (control) => ValidationErrors | null. An async validator returns an Observable/Promise of the same — used for server checks like 'is this username taken?'. Attach them when building the control.
tip: Async validators debounce well with the updateOn: 'blur' option to avoid hammering the server.

question: How do you read route params — snapshot vs observable?
answer: Inject ActivatedRoute. Use snapshot.paramMap for a one-time read. Subscribe to paramMap (Observable) when the same component instance is reused across param changes (e.g. navigating /user/1 → /user/2) so it reacts to the new id.
tip: If your detail page doesn't refresh when the id changes, you used snapshot where you needed the observable.
code:
this.route.paramMap.subscribe(p => this.load(p.get('id')));
// query params: this.route.queryParamMap

question: What does a modern functional route guard look like?
answer: Since v14+, guards are plain functions (CanActivateFn) that use inject() — no class needed. They return true, false, or a UrlTree to redirect.
tip: Returning a UrlTree from a guard is the clean way to redirect (e.g. to /login).

question: How do you handle HttpClient errors and retries?
answer: Pipe the request through retry(n) for transient failures and catchError to map/handle the error (log, show a message, return a fallback). Centralise cross-cutting handling in an HTTP interceptor.
tip: retry then catchError is the standard resilience pattern for flaky endpoints.
code:
this.http.get<User>(url).pipe(
  retry(2),
  catchError(err => { this.notify(err); return of(null); })
)

question: What are route preloading strategies?
answer: Lazy routes load on demand. A preloading strategy fetches some lazy bundles in the background after the app boots, so navigation feels instant. Built-in PreloadAllModules, or a custom strategy that preloads only routes flagged in their data.
tip: PreloadAllModules gives lazy-loading's small initial bundle plus fast later navigation.
code: provideRouter(routes, withPreloading(PreloadAllModules))

question: ViewChild vs ViewChildren (and QueryList).
answer: @ViewChild returns a single reference; @ViewChildren returns a QueryList of all matches, which is live — subscribe to changes to react when items are added/removed. @ContentChildren is the projected-content equivalent.
tip: QueryList.changes lets you respond to dynamically added children.
code:
@ViewChildren(ItemComponent) items!: QueryList<ItemComponent>;
ngAfterViewInit() { this.items.changes.subscribe(() => {}); }

question: How do you load a component dynamically?
answer: Use ViewContainerRef.createComponent() to instantiate a component at runtime (modals, widget dashboards, plugin systems). Modern Angular takes the component class directly — the old ComponentFactoryResolver is deprecated.
tip: Set inputs via componentRef.setInput() and clean up with componentRef.destroy().
code:
const ref = this.vcr.createComponent(MyWidgetComponent);
ref.setInput('title', 'Hello');

question: How does Angular's animation system work?
answer: From @angular/animations: define a trigger with states and transitions (using style + animate), then bind it in the template with [@triggerName]. Handy built-ins: :enter / :leave for add/remove.
tip: For simple hover/fade effects, plain CSS transitions are lighter than the animations package.

question: What is SSR / Angular Universal, and hydration?
answer: SSR renders the app to HTML on the server per request — better SEO and faster first contentful paint. Hydration (v16+, non-destructive) reuses that server-rendered DOM on the client instead of re-rendering, avoiding a flicker.
tip: SSR is why you must avoid direct document/window access — use Renderer2 and isPlatformBrowser.
code:
// enable non-destructive hydration:
provideClientHydration()

question: What are deferrable views (@defer)?
answer: (v17+) @defer lazily loads a block of template and its dependencies based on triggers — on viewport, on idle, on interaction, on hover, etc. It shrinks the initial bundle with a declarative, template-level API and supports @placeholder/@loading/@error.
tip: @defer is the modern, template-level way to code-split heavy widgets like charts.

question: What are signal inputs, model(), and signal queries?
answer: (v17.1+) The signal-based reactivity APIs: input() replaces @Input (with input.required() and transforms), model() creates a two-way bindable signal, and viewChild()/contentChild() return signal queries. They compose cleanly with computed and effect.
tip: Signal inputs remove the ngOnChanges dance — derive with computed() instead.

question: How does Angular protect against XSS?
answer: Angular treats all binding values as untrusted and sanitizes them by context (HTML, style, URL). Interpolation escapes HTML automatically. To render trusted HTML you must explicitly opt in via DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrust… — use it sparingly and only on values you control.
tip: If you're reaching for bypassSecurityTrustHtml, double-check the source is truly trusted.
code: // safe by default — Angular sanitizes bound HTML:

question: Smart (container) vs dumb (presentational) components.
answer: Smart/container components fetch data, hold state, and talk to services/the store. Dumb/presentational components just take @Inputs and emit @Outputs — no dependencies, easy to test and reuse. This separation keeps UI reusable and logic centralised.
tip: Presentational components pair perfectly with OnPush since they're pure input → view.

question: What is ControlValueAccessor and when do you implement it?
answer:
ControlValueAccessor is the bridge that lets a custom component act as a native form control — usable with [(ngModel)], formControlName, and validation. You implement four methods:

  • writeValue(v) — model → view (Angular sets the value).
  • registerOnChange(fn) — view → model (call fn when the user changes it).
  • registerOnTouched(fn) — mark as touched (on blur).
  • setDisabledState() — react to disabling.

Register it with the NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR multi-provider.
tip: This is THE classic advanced-forms question — a custom rating/toggle/date-picker uses it.

question: How do you validate across multiple fields (e.g. password match)?
answer: Put a group-level validator on the FormGroup instead of a single control, so it can compare siblings. Return an error on the group (or set it on a specific control) when the rule fails.
tip: Cross-field rules like password-confirm belong on the FormGroup, not one control.

question: What are typed reactive forms (v14+)?
answer: Since v14, reactive forms are strictly typed: FormGroup/FormControl infer value types, so form.value and .get() are type-safe and autocompleted. Use FormControl<string> and nonNullable to avoid null in the type.
tip: nonNullable: true keeps the control's type as string instead of string | null.
code:
email = new FormControl('', { nonNullable: true, validators: [Validators.email] });
// email.value is string, not string | null

question: How do you react to form changes reactively?
answer: Every control/group exposes valueChanges and statusChanges as Observables. Pipe them (e.g. debounceTime + switchMap) for autosave, dependent fields, or live search. Set updateOn: 'blur' to reduce noise.
tip: valueChanges + debounceTime is the clean autosave / dependent-field pattern.

question: What does updateOn: 'blur' | 'submit' do?
answer: Controls the moment validation and valueChanges fire. Default is 'change' (every keystroke). 'blur' validates when the field loses focus, and 'submit' only on submit — reducing churn and improving UX for expensive/async validators.
tip: updateOn: 'blur' is a simple way to stop async validators firing on every keystroke.
code: new FormControl('', { updateOn: 'blur', validators: [Validators.required] });

question: What is a selector in Angular and why use it?
answer:
The selector is a property in the @Component decorator that defines how the component is identified in a template. It acts like a custom HTML tag, so Angular knows where to render the component.

Selectors can be an element (app-user), an attribute ([appHighlight]), or a class.
tip: Prefix your selectors (app-…) so they never clash with real or future HTML elements.
code:
@Component({ selector: 'app-user', templateUrl: './user.html' })
// used as: <app-user></app-user>

question: What is the entry point of an Angular application?
answer: main.ts is the entry point. It bootstraps the app — historically platformBrowserDynamic().bootstrapModule(AppModule), and in modern standalone Angular bootstrapApplication(AppComponent). This initialises the root and renders the app into the browser.
tip: Bootstrapping = 'start the app and render the root component'. Know both the module and standalone forms.
code:
// modern standalone bootstrap
bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, { providers: [provideRouter(routes)] });

question: What is the use of router-outlet?
answer: <router-outlet> is a placeholder in a template where the router renders the component that matches the current route. As the URL changes, Angular swaps the component shown at that outlet. You can also have named/nested outlets.
tip: Think of router-outlet as the 'stage' where routed components are displayed.
code:
<nav>…</nav>
<router-outlet></router-outlet> <!-- routed component renders here -->

question: How do you call a REST API in Angular?
answer: Use the HttpClient service (provide it with provideHttpClient()). Wrap the calls in a dedicated service that returns Observables, and handle the HTTP verbs (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) with proper error handling via catchError. Components subscribe (ideally through the async pipe).
tip: Keep API calls in a service, not the component — it's reusable, testable, and mockable.
deep:
What is HttpClient?

HttpClient is Angular's built-in service for communicating with REST APIs. It supports all HTTP methods such as GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE. Every request returns an Observable, allowing asynchronous handling, retries, cancellation, and RxJS operators.

Typical Flow

Component
     ↓
UserService
     ↓
HttpClient
     ↓
REST API
     ↓
Database

The component should never call HttpClient directly. Instead, API calls are encapsulated inside a service to keep the application modular, reusable, and easy to test.

1. Configure HttpClient

In standalone Angular applications, enable it using provideHttpClient().

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, {
  providers: [provideHttpClient()]
});

2. Create a Service

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class UserService {
  constructor(private http: HttpClient) {}

  getUsers() {
    return this.http.get<User[]>('/api/users');
  }

  createUser(user: User) {
    return this.http.post<User>('/api/users', user);
  }
}

3. Consume in Component

export class UsersComponent {
  users$ = this.userService.getUsers();

  constructor(private userService: UserService) {}
}
<ul>
  <li *ngFor="let user of users$ | async">
    {{ user.name }}
  </li>
</ul>

Using the async pipe automatically subscribes and unsubscribes from the Observable.

HTTP Methods

MethodPurpose
GETRetrieve data
POSTCreate new resource
PUTReplace existing resource
PATCHUpdate part of a resource
DELETERemove resource

Error Handling

Use RxJS operators like catchError to handle server errors in the service instead of the component.

getUsers() {
  return this.http.get<User[]>('/api/users').pipe(
    catchError(err => {
      console.error(err);
      return throwError(() => err);
    })
  );
}

Real-world Example

Imagine a Customer Management application.

  1. The user opens the Customer page.
  2. The component calls customerService.getCustomers().
  3. The service sends a GET request using HttpClient.
  4. The backend returns customer data.
  5. The Observable emits the response.
  6. The UI updates automatically using the async pipe.
CustomerComponent
        ↓
CustomerService
        ↓
GET /api/customers
        ↓
Spring Boot API
        ↓
PostgreSQL

Http Interceptors

Enterprise applications rarely attach headers in every service method. Instead, an HttpInterceptor automatically adds common information such as JWT tokens, correlation IDs, or logs every request.

export const authInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  const token = localStorage.getItem('token');

  const authReq = req.clone({
    setHeaders: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`
    }
  });

  return next(authReq);
};

Best Practices

  • Keep all API calls inside services.
  • Return Observables instead of subscribing inside services.
  • Use the async pipe whenever possible.
  • Handle errors with catchError.
  • Use Interceptors for authentication headers and common request logic.
  • Strongly type API responses using TypeScript interfaces.

Interview Answer

"In Angular, REST APIs are called using HttpClient. We usually create a dedicated service that returns Observables and keeps HTTP logic separate from the UI. Components consume these Observables using the async pipe or subscribe when necessary. Error handling is done with RxJS operators like catchError, and enterprise applications typically use Http Interceptors to attach authentication tokens and handle common request logic."

question: How do you make an API call that depends on another call's response?
answer: Chain them with switchMap so the second request uses the first's result (and stale first-calls are cancelled). For independent parallel calls use forkJoin; for ordered writes use concatMap.
tip: Dependent = switchMap/concatMap; independent-parallel = forkJoin.

question: What is the difference between a component and a directive?
answer: A component is a directive with a template — it controls a view. A directive has no template; it adds behaviour or changes the appearance/structure of existing elements (attribute directives like ngClass, structural directives like *ngIf).
tip: One-liner: 'A component is a directive with a template.'
deep:
The relationship

All three are directives — a component is just the special case that owns a view:

TypeTemplate?Purpose
Componentyescontrols a piece of UI (view + logic)
Attribute directivenochanges appearance/behaviour of an existing element
Structural directivenoadds/removes elements from the DOM

Component

@Component({ selector: 'app-user', template: '<h2>{{ name }}</h2>' })
export class UserComponent { name = 'Asha'; }

Attribute directive — behaviour, no template

@Directive({ selector: '[appHighlight]' })
export class HighlightDirective {
  constructor(el: ElementRef) { el.nativeElement.style.background = 'yellow'; }
}
// <p appHighlight>...</p>

Structural directive — changes DOM layout

*ngIf, *ngFor (and the v17 @if/@for) add or remove elements. The * is sugar for wrapping the element in an <ng-template>.

How to say it in an interview

“A component is a directive with a template — it renders a view. A directive has no template; attribute directives change how an existing element looks or behaves, and structural directives add or remove elements. Every component is a directive, but not every directive is a component.”

question: Why do we use services in Angular?
answer:
• Encapsulate reusable business logic and API calls.
  • Share data/state between components (often via a BehaviorSubject).
  • Provide a single instance (singleton) through dependency injection.
  • Enforce separation of concerns — keep components focused on the view.
tip: Components render; services do the work. Keep logic out of components.

question: What are Angular route guards and their types?
answer:
Guards control access to and navigation between routes.

  • CanActivate — allow/deny entering a route (auth).
  • CanDeactivate — confirm leaving (e.g. unsaved-changes prompt).
  • Resolve — pre-fetch data before the route activates.
  • CanMatch / CanLoad — control whether a lazy route is even loaded.
tip: For auth, a CanActivate guard returning a UrlTree is the clean redirect-to-login pattern.
code:
export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = () =>
  inject(AuthService).isLoggedIn() || inject(Router).createUrlTree(['/login']);
deep:
The guard types

GuardRunsTypical use
CanActivatebefore entering a routeauth / role check
CanActivateChildbefore entering any child routeprotect a whole feature area
CanDeactivatebefore leaving a route“You have unsaved changes” prompt
CanMatchbefore a route is even matchedfeature flags, role-based route swapping, block lazy load
Resolveafter activation is allowed, before renderpre-fetch data

Note: CanLoad is deprecated in favour of CanMatch, which also blocks the lazy chunk from loading.

Return types

A guard returns boolean, a UrlTree (redirect), or an Observable/Promise of those. Returning a UrlTree is the clean way to redirect:

export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = (route, state) => {
  const auth = inject(AuthService);
  const router = inject(Router);
  return auth.isLoggedIn()
    ? true
    : router.createUrlTree(['/login'], { queryParams: { returnUrl: state.url } });
};

Functional guards (modern) vs class guards (legacy)

Since v14, guards are plain functions (CanActivateFn) using inject() — less boilerplate than the old class-based implements CanActivate. Wire them in the route config:

{ path: 'admin', canActivate: [authGuard], loadComponent: ... }
{ path: 'edit', canDeactivate: [unsavedChangesGuard] }

CanDeactivate pattern

export const unsavedChangesGuard: CanDeactivateFn<EditComponent> =
  (component) => component.form.pristine || confirm('Discard changes?');

How to say it in an interview

“Guards gate navigation. CanActivate/CanActivateChild protect entering routes, CanDeactivate confirms leaving, CanMatch decides whether a route (including its lazy bundle) is used at all, and Resolve pre-fetches data. Modern guards are functions using inject(), and returning a UrlTree is the idiomatic redirect.”

question: How would you debug "Cannot read properties of undefined" in Angular?
answer:
It means you accessed a property on something that isn't ready/loaded yet (often async data rendered before it arrives). Fixes:

  • Guard the template with *ngIf="data" or the safe-navigation operator data?.name.
  • Initialise the variable before use; check the async flow with the async pipe.
  • Use the browser devtools/stack trace to find the exact line and inspect the value.
tip: The template safe-navigation operator (?.) prevents this for async data that hasn't loaded yet.
code:
{{ user?.profile?.name }}
{{ u.name }}

question: What are the options for state management in Angular?
answer:
• Service + BehaviorSubject — simple shared state for small/medium apps.
  • Signals — modern, fine-grained local/shared state.
  • NgRx / NgRx SignalStore — structured Redux-style store for large apps needing traceability and DevTools.
tip: Match the tool to the app size — don't reach for NgRx on a small app.
deep:
The spectrum, smallest to largest

OptionGood forTrade-off
Component state / @Inputlocal, single componentdoesn't scale to shared state
Service + BehaviorSubjectsmall–medium shared statemanual, boilerplate for complex flows
Service + Signalsmodern shared/local statenewer; async still needs RxJS
NgRx SignalStorestructured store, less boilerplate than classic NgRxlearning curve
NgRx (Store/Effects)large apps needing traceability, DevTools, time-travelmost boilerplate

Service + BehaviorSubject — the workhorse

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class CartService {
  private items$ = new BehaviorSubject<Item[]>([]);
  readonly items = this.items$.asObservable();
  add(i: Item) { this.items$.next([...this.items$.value, i]); }
}

Expose a read-only stream, mutate through methods. This covers a surprising amount of real-world apps.

Signal-based store

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class CounterStore {
  private _count = signal(0);
  count = this._count.asReadonly();
  double = computed(() => this._count() * 2);
  inc() { this._count.update(n => n + 1); }
}

When NgRx earns its keep

Reach for NgRx when many components share complex state, you need an audit trail of how state changed (actions), time-travel debugging, or well-defined side-effect handling (Effects). Its cost is boilerplate — actions, reducers, selectors, effects — so it's overkill for small apps.

How to say it in an interview

“I scale the tool to the app. Local state stays in the component; shared state goes in a service with a BehaviorSubject or signals. I only introduce NgRx (or SignalStore) when the app is large enough to need structured actions, traceability, and DevTools — otherwise it’s just boilerplate.”

question: constructor vs ngOnInit — what's the difference?
answer:
The constructor is a TypeScript/JS class feature that runs when the class is instantiated — Angular uses it only to inject dependencies. ngOnInit is an Angular lifecycle hook that runs after the first change detection, once @Input values are set.

Rule of thumb: inject in the constructor, initialise in ngOnInit (data fetching, setup that needs inputs).
tip: @Input values are undefined in the constructor — that alone is why real init belongs in ngOnInit.
code:
constructor(private api: ApiService) {}   // DI only
ngOnInit() { this.user = this.api.getUser(this.id); } // id is ready here
deep:
Why two places at all?

The constructor fires the instant Angular creates the component object — before it has wired up bindings or rendered anything. So @Input properties are still undefined and the DOM/@ViewChild don't exist yet. ngOnInit runs a beat later, after the first ngOnChanges, so inputs are populated.

constructorngOnInit
Belongs tothe class (TS)Angular lifecycle
Runson instantiationafter first ngOnChanges
@Input ready?noyes
Use fordependency injectioninit logic, data fetch
@Input() id!: string;
constructor(private api: ApiService) {
  console.log(this.id); // undefined 😖
}
ngOnInit() {
  console.log(this.id); // 'abc' ✅
  this.api.getUser(this.id).subscribe(u => this.user = u);
}

How to say it in an interview

“The constructor is a class feature Angular uses for dependency injection — inputs aren’t set yet. ngOnInit runs after the first change detection when inputs are available, so I do initialization and data fetching there.”

question: What is trackBy in *ngFor and why use it?
answer: By default *ngFor tracks list items by object identity, so if the array reference changes (e.g. after an HTTP refresh) Angular destroys and recreates every DOM node. A trackBy function tells Angular how to identify an item (usually by id), so it only re-renders the rows that actually changed.
tip: trackBy is the fix for a list that flickers or loses input focus when the data reloads.
code:
• {{ u.name }}
    • trackById(index: number, u: User) { return u.id; } // v17 control flow: @for (u of users; track u.id) { ... }
deep:
The problem it solves

When you reassign a list (this.users = [...fresh]), the new objects have new references. Without trackBy, Angular can't tell they represent the same rows, so it tears down and rebuilds the whole list — expensive, and it drops DOM state like scroll position, focus, or CSS animations.

How trackBy fixes it

Return a stable unique key per item. Angular then diffs by that key and touches only added/removed/changed rows.

trackById(index: number, item: User): number {
  return item.id;
}

In the v17+ control flow, track is mandatory: @for (u of users; track u.id) { }.

How to say it in an interview

“trackBy gives each item a stable identity so *ngFor reuses DOM nodes instead of rebuilding the whole list when the array reference changes — a big win for large or frequently-refreshed lists.”

question: forRoot() vs forChild() — what's the difference?
answer:
A pattern for modules that provide services. forRoot() is called once in the root/app module and registers the module's providers (singletons). forChild() is called in feature modules and registers routes/components without re-creating those providers.

Classic example: RouterModule.forRoot(routes) at the app root, RouterModule.forChild(routes) in each lazy feature.
tip: Calling forRoot() twice can create duplicate service instances — that's exactly the bug the pattern prevents.
code:
// app: RouterModule.forRoot(appRoutes)
// feature: RouterModule.forChild(featureRoutes)

question: What causes ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenCheckedError?
answer: In dev mode Angular runs change detection twice and compares. If a bound value changed between the two passes — usually because you mutated it in a lifecycle hook like ngAfterViewInit that runs after the view was already checked — it throws this error to warn of an inconsistent view.
tip: It only appears in dev mode — but it signals a real bug: your view and model briefly disagreed.
deep:
Why it happens

To guarantee the rendered DOM matches the model, dev-mode Angular does a second change-detection pass right after the first and checks that nothing changed. If a value updated in between, the two passes disagree and Angular throws — because in production (single pass) the user would see a stale value.

Typical trigger

@ViewChild('box') box!: ElementRef;
ngAfterViewInit() {
  this.height = this.box.nativeElement.offsetHeight; // bound in the template
} // ❌ view was already checked this cycle

Fixes

  • Move the change to an earlier hook (ngOnInit) if possible.
  • Defer it to the next tick: setTimeout(() => this.height = h) or Promise.resolve().then(...).
  • Force a re-check: inject ChangeDetectorRef and call detectChanges() after updating.

How to say it in an interview

“It’s a dev-mode guard: Angular checks the view twice and a bound value changed after the first check — usually from updating state in ngAfterViewInit. I fix it by moving the update earlier, deferring it a tick, or calling detectChanges().”

question: Observable vs Promise — what's the difference?
answer:
• Promise — a single future value; eager (runs immediately); not cancellable; no operators.
  • Observable — a stream of 0..∞ values over time; lazy (runs only on subscribe); cancellable via unsubscribe; rich operators (map, switchMap, debounceTime).

Angular's HttpClient returns Observables, which is why they dominate Angular apps.
tip: Say the three big wins: multiple values, lazy, and cancellable — plus operators.
deep:
Side-by-side

PromiseObservable
Valuesexactly onezero to many, over time
Eager/lazyeager (runs on creation)lazy (runs on subscribe)
Cancelnoyes (unsubscribe)
Operatorsnonemap, filter, switchMap…
Retrymanualretry() operator

Lazy matters

A Promise starts its work the moment it's created. An Observable does nothing until something subscribes — so an unsubscribed HTTP Observable never even fires the request. That laziness is what enables cancellation and operators like switchMap (cancel the previous request when a new one starts).

// typeahead: cancel stale requests automatically
this.query$.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  switchMap(q => this.http.get(`/search?q=${q}`))
).subscribe(...);

Interop

Convert with firstValueFrom(obs) (Observable→Promise) or from(promise) (Promise→Observable).

How to say it in an interview

“A Promise resolves once, eagerly, and can’t be cancelled. An Observable is a lazy, cancellable stream of many values with a big operator library — which is why Angular’s HttpClient and reactive forms use Observables.”

question: How would you handle rapid user actions (e.g. live search or AI analysis requests) to avoid multiple API calls and outdated results?
answer: Use debounceTime() to wait until the user stops typing or clicking for a short duration, reducing unnecessary API calls. Then use switchMap() to automatically cancel any previous pending request whenever a new request is triggered. This ensures that only the latest response is processed and displayed.
tip: Remember: debounceTime delays the request, switchMap cancels the previous request.
deep:
Real-world scenario

Imagine an AI dashboard where the user clicks different timestamps on a video to request AI analysis. Each click triggers an HTTP request.

If the user clicks Timestamp A, then immediately clicks Timestamp B, you don't want the response from A arriving after B and overwriting the latest result.

switchMap() automatically unsubscribes from (cancels) the previous HTTP request and subscribes only to the latest one, ensuring stale data is ignored.

Role of debounceTime()

debounceTime(300) waits until the user pauses for 300ms before sending the request. This prevents flooding the server with unnecessary API calls during rapid typing or clicking.

Why switchMap?

OperatorBehavior
switchMapCancels previous request and keeps only the latest.
mergeMapRuns all requests concurrently.
concatMapQueues requests and executes one after another.
exhaustMapIgnores new requests until the current one finishes.

How to answer in an interview

“For rapid user interactions like live search or AI analysis, I'd combine debounceTime() and switchMap(). debounceTime reduces unnecessary API calls, while switchMap automatically cancels previous requests so only the latest response updates the UI. This prevents race conditions and keeps the frontend responsive.”

question: Your page displays 10,000 records and scrolling becomes very slow. How would you optimize it?
answer: First, use trackBy so Angular reuses existing DOM elements instead of recreating them. For very large datasets, use CDK Virtual Scroll so only the visible items are rendered. Combine this with ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush to reduce unnecessary change detection.
tip: The three keywords interviewers expect are: trackBy, Virtual Scroll, and OnPush.
code:
{{ user.name }}
trackById(index: number, user: User) { return user.id; }
deep:
Real-world scenario

A dashboard loads thousands of customer records. Without optimization Angular creates thousands of DOM elements, making scrolling slow.

Solution

  • Use trackBy to reuse DOM elements.
  • Use CDK Virtual Scroll to render only visible rows.
  • Use OnPush to reduce change detection.

Interview answer

"I first reduce DOM creation using trackBy. If the dataset is huge, I implement Angular CDK Virtual Scroll so only visible rows exist in the DOM. Finally, I use OnPush to minimize unnecessary change detection."

question: How do you prevent memory leaks caused by RxJS subscriptions?
answer: Always unsubscribe from long-lived Observables when the component is destroyed. In modern Angular, the recommended approach is using takeUntilDestroyed(). In older versions, use takeUntil() with a Subject or rely on the async pipe whenever possible.
tip: The Async Pipe automatically subscribes and unsubscribes for you.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A component subscribes to WebSocket updates. Every time the user navigates away and back, another subscription is created, causing duplicate events and increasing memory usage.

Best practices

  • Prefer the Async Pipe in templates.
  • Use takeUntilDestroyed() for manual subscriptions.
  • Avoid storing unnecessary subscriptions.

Interview answer

"I avoid memory leaks by preferring the Async Pipe. For manual subscriptions I use takeUntilDestroyed(), ensuring Angular automatically cleans them up when the component is destroyed."

question: How would you prevent users from submitting the same form multiple times by clicking the Save button repeatedly?
answer: Disable the Save button while the request is in progress or use exhaustMap(). It ignores subsequent button clicks until the current request completes, preventing duplicate submissions.
tip: Remember: switchMap cancels previous, exhaustMap ignores new requests.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A user clicks Save five times because the API is slow. Without protection, five identical orders may be created.

Solution

  • Disable the Save button.
  • Or use exhaustMap() so additional clicks are ignored until the first request completes.

Interview answer

"For Save buttons I usually combine a loading state with exhaustMap(), ensuring duplicate submissions are ignored while the current request is running."

question: How would you share data between unrelated Angular components?
answer: For unrelated components, avoid directly referencing each other. Use a shared service with an RxJS BehaviorSubject or use NgRx if the state is shared across multiple features. Parent-child communication should still use @Input() and @Output().
tip: The communication mechanism depends on the relationship between components.
deep:
Communication choices

ScenarioRecommended Approach
Parent → Child@Input()
Child → Parent@Output()
Unrelated ComponentsShared Service + BehaviorSubject
Large ApplicationNgRx Store

Interview answer

"I choose the communication mechanism based on the relationship between components. Parent-child uses Input/Output, unrelated components use a shared service, and application-wide shared state uses NgRx."

question: Your Angular application has become slow because the initial bundle size is very large. How would you improve the loading performance?
answer: I would split the application into feature modules and lazy load them using the Angular Router. This ensures only the modules required for the current route are downloaded initially, reducing the bundle size and improving the application's startup time.
tip: The first optimization interviewers expect is Lazy Loading.
deep:
Real-world scenario

Your application has 15 modules, but users usually access only 2 or 3 of them. Loading everything during startup increases the bundle size and slows down the application.

Solution

  • Use Lazy Loading for feature modules.
  • Preload frequently used modules if necessary.
  • Keep the initial bundle as small as possible.

Interview answer

"I split the application into feature modules and lazy load them using the router. This reduces the initial bundle size, improves startup time, and downloads features only when users navigate to them."

question: Multiple components call the same API, causing duplicate network requests. How would you optimize it?
answer: I would cache the API response inside a shared service using shareReplay(1). The first component triggers the HTTP request, while subsequent subscribers receive the cached response without making additional network calls.
tip: shareReplay(1) is the most common RxJS operator used for HTTP caching.
deep:
Real-world scenario

The Header, Dashboard and Profile components all request the logged-in user. Without caching, three identical HTTP requests are sent.

Solution

Store the Observable in a service and use shareReplay(1) so every component receives the same cached result.

Interview answer

"I'd cache the HTTP Observable using shareReplay(1), ensuring the API is called only once while all components share the same response."

question: How would you prevent unauthenticated users from accessing protected pages?
