answer: I would use an Angular Route Guard such as CanActivate. Before navigation, the guard checks whether the user is authenticated. If not, it redirects the user to the login page.
tip: Authentication checks belong in Route Guards, not inside every component.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A user manually enters the URL '/admin'. Even though the menu is hidden, they should still be prevented from accessing the page.

Solution

Use a Route Guard to verify authentication before navigation.

Interview answer

"Instead of checking authentication inside every component, I centralize the logic using a Route Guard, ensuring protected routes cannot be accessed directly."

question: A component is re-rendering frequently and performance is poor. How would you optimize Angular Change Detection?
answer: I would use ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush. With OnPush, Angular checks the component only when an @Input() reference changes, an event occurs, or an Observable emits. This significantly reduces unnecessary change detection cycles.
tip: OnPush works best with immutable objects and the Async Pipe.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A dashboard contains many widgets that refresh frequently. Default change detection causes every component to be checked after each event, reducing performance.

Solution

  • Use OnPush.
  • Avoid mutating objects directly.
  • Prefer immutable updates and the Async Pipe.

Interview answer

"I'd switch the component to OnPush so Angular performs change detection only when necessary. Combined with immutable data and the Async Pipe, this greatly improves rendering performance."

question: How would you implement an auto-save feature for a form without sending an API request on every keystroke?
answer: I would subscribe to form.valueChanges, apply debounceTime() to wait until the user stops typing, use distinctUntilChanged() to avoid duplicate requests, and finally use switchMap() to cancel any pending save request when new changes occur.
tip: Auto-save is one of the best real-world examples combining debounceTime, distinctUntilChanged and switchMap.
deep:
Real-world scenario

While writing a document or filling a long form, users expect their work to be saved automatically without clicking a Save button.

Solution

  • Listen to valueChanges.
  • Wait until the user pauses typing.
  • Ignore duplicate values.
  • Cancel previous save requests if new edits occur.

Interview answer

"I'd use valueChanges with debounceTime(), distinctUntilChanged() and switchMap() so drafts are saved efficiently without flooding the server."

question: How would you handle HTTP errors consistently across the entire Angular application?
answer: I would create an HTTP Interceptor. It centralizes error handling, logging, authentication failures, and token refresh logic instead of duplicating the same code inside every component or service.
tip: Cross-cutting concerns like authentication and error handling belong in an Interceptor.
deep:
Real-world scenario

Every API call should display consistent error messages and automatically redirect users when a 401 Unauthorized response is received.

Interview answer

"Instead of handling errors in every component, I'd centralize them using an HTTP Interceptor. This keeps the code cleaner and ensures consistent behavior throughout the application."

question: How would you handle an expired JWT token without forcing the user to log in again?
answer: I would detect a 401 Unauthorized response inside an HTTP Interceptor, call the Refresh Token API, store the new access token, retry the original request, and only redirect to login if the refresh request also fails.
tip: The retry logic should be implemented inside an HTTP Interceptor, not in every API call.
code:
401 Error
↓
Refresh Token API
↓
New Access Token
↓
Retry Original Request
deep:
Real-world scenario

A user's access token expires after one hour, but the refresh token is still valid. The user should continue working without interruption.

Interview answer

"I'd intercept 401 responses, refresh the token transparently, retry the failed request, and only redirect to login if refreshing the token also fails."

question: How would you display upload progress while uploading a large file?
answer: Angular's HttpClient supports upload progress events. By enabling reportProgress and observe: 'events', I can calculate the upload percentage and display a progress bar to the user.
tip: Always use upload progress for large files to improve user experience.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A user uploads a 500 MB video. Without progress feedback, the application appears frozen and users may retry the upload.

Interview answer

"I'd use HttpClient's progress events to calculate the upload percentage and display a progress bar, giving users clear feedback during long uploads."

question: What are microfrontends and how do they differ from a normal frontend?
answer:
A microfrontend architecture splits one web app into independently built, tested and deployed pieces, each owned end-to-end by a different team, and composed together in the browser at runtime.

A normal (monolithic) frontend is one codebase, one build, one deploy — every team's change ships in the same bundle, on the same release train.

The real difference isn't technical, it's organisational: microfrontends trade runtime and tooling complexity for independent deployability. In Angular this is usually done with Module Federation (Webpack 5 / Native Federation), where a shell loads remotes at runtime.
tip: The follow-up is always 'when would you NOT use them?' — if you have one team and one release train, microfrontends are pure cost with no benefit. Say that out loud; it's the senior signal.
deep:
Monolith vs microfrontend

Monolithic frontendMicrofrontends
Buildoneone per remote
Deployall teams togetherindependently
Reposingle (or Nx monorepo)often one per team
Tech choiceone Angular versioncan differ (at a cost)
Blast radiusa bad merge blocks everyoneisolated to one remote
Runtime costnoneversion skew, duplicate deps
Best for1–3 teamsmany teams, one product

The problem they actually solve

Not performance — team autonomy. At ~5+ teams in one repo, the release train becomes the bottleneck: your finished feature waits on someone else's failing test, merge conflicts multiply, and a regression anywhere blocks every deploy. Microfrontends let the Orders team ship at 2pm without asking the Payments team.

If you don't have that pain, you don't have the problem they solve.

How composition works

  • Build-time (npm packages) — simple, but a remote change needs a shell rebuild + redeploy. That defeats the point.
  • Runtime via Module Federation ← the standard answer. The shell fetches remoteEntry.js at runtime, so the remote deploys on its own.
  • iframes — bulletproof isolation, painful UX (routing, sizing, modals, auth).
  • Web Components — framework-agnostic wrapper, often combined with the above.

The hard parts (this is where interviews go)

1. Shared dependencies. Three remotes each bundling Angular means shipping it three times. Mark framework packages singleton: true so one copy is shared — but then all remotes must agree on a compatible version, which quietly re-couples the teams you just decoupled.

2. The Router must be a singleton. Two Angular Router instances fighting over the URL is the classic microfrontend bug.

3. Shared state & auth. Don't share a store across remotes — that's coupling by the back door. Keep the token in the shell (or a cookie) and pass context down via inputs/custom events. Cross-remote communication should be coarse events, not shared objects.

4. Version skew. The shell and remotes are deployed at different times, so any combination can be live simultaneously. Contracts must be backward compatible.

5. Consistent UX. Independent teams drift visually — a shared design system (published as a library) is mandatory, not optional.

In Angular specifically

ng add @angular-architects/module-federation --project shell   --port 4200
ng add @angular-architects/module-federation --project orders  --port 4201

For modern Angular (esbuild/Vite builder), Webpack Module Federation doesn't apply — use Native Federation (@angular-architects/native-federation), which is the same idea on browser-native ES modules and import maps.

When NOT to use them

  • One or two teams — you get every cost and no benefit. An Nx monorepo with enforced module boundaries gives you most of the structure with none of the runtime pain.
  • A tightly-coupled UI where remotes constantly share state — you'd build a distributed monolith: all the complexity, none of the independence.
  • Performance-critical pages — duplicate deps and extra round-trips make bundles bigger, not smaller.

How to say it in an interview

“Microfrontends split the app into independently deployable pieces owned by different teams, composed at runtime — in Angular, usually Module Federation with a shell loading remotes. The point isn't performance, it's removing the shared release train once you have enough teams that it's a bottleneck. The costs are real though: framework packages have to be shared singletons, the Router must be a singleton, and since shell and remotes deploy independently you have to treat their contracts as backward-compatible APIs. For one or two teams I'd use an Nx monorepo with enforced boundaries instead — same structure, no runtime cost.”

question: How do you build a scalable enterprise Angular application?
answer:
First, define the axis — this is the trap. On a frontend, "scalable" almost never means server traffic; it means the codebase and the team can keep growing without the app rotting.

  • Code scale — feature-based structure with enforced boundaries, so 200k lines stay navigable.
  • Team scale — clear ownership; teams don't collide in the same files.
  • Runtime scale — lazy loading, OnPush, virtual scrolling, performance budgets so it stays fast as features pile on.
  • Data scale — pagination and virtualisation; never render 10k rows.
tip: Ask 'scalable in what sense?' or state your axis explicitly. Candidates who drift into load balancers and DB sharding for a FRONTEND question expose that the resume bullet was filler.
deep:
Step 1 — disambiguate, don't ramble

“Scalable” is the vaguest word on most resumes, so the interviewer is checking whether you know what you meant. Name the axis first. For an enterprise Angular app the honest answer is usually: the codebase and the number of teams touching it — plus keeping runtime performance flat as it grows.

Code scale — structure by feature, not by type

// Doesn't scale — every feature dumps into shared folders
src/app/components/  services/  models/

// Scales — a feature is a deletable, ownable unit
src/app/
  core/                 # singletons: auth, interceptors, guards
  shared/               # dumb reusable UI, pipes
  features/
    orders/             # everything Orders, nothing else imports its internals
      data-access/
      feature-list/
      ui/

The test: can I delete this folder and only this feature breaks? If deleting Orders breaks Billing, you don't have a boundary.

Boundaries have to be enforced by tooling

A structure convention decays the moment someone is in a hurry. In an Nx monorepo, tag libraries and let @nx/enforce-module-boundaries fail the build on a cross-feature import. That converts “please don't import that” into a lint error — the difference between an architecture and a wish.

Team scale

  • CODEOWNERS per feature folder — reviews route automatically.
  • A shared design system as its own library, or every team reinvents the button.
  • Nx affected — only rebuild/retest what changed, so CI doesn't grow linearly with the repo.
  • Microfrontends only when the shared release train genuinely becomes the bottleneck (see microfrontends).

Runtime scale — what actually degrades

GrowsSymptomFix
Featuresinitial bundle bloatslazy-loaded routes, @defer, budgets in angular.json
Components on screenevery CD cycle checks everythingOnPush + signals
Rows of dataDOM chokesvirtual scroll + trackBy, server-side paging
Subscriptionsmemory leak, slow over timetakeUntilDestroyed / async pipe
// A budget makes bundle regressions fail CI instead of shipping
"budgets": [{ "type": "initial", "maximumError": "1mb" }]

State — the thing that rots first

Don't reach for a global store reflexively. Keep state as local as possible; promote to a store only when genuinely shared. A store that every feature writes to is a global variable with ceremony — and it re-couples the features you carefully separated.

What makes it "enterprise"

Usually the non-functional requirements, and they're worth naming: role-based access control, audit trails, i18n/l10n, accessibility compliance, SSO, long-term browser support, and release processes with sign-off. Mentioning these shows you've worked on real enterprise software rather than a large CRUD app.

How to say it in an interview

“First I'd clarify the axis — on the frontend, scaling usually means the codebase and the team, not traffic. I structure by feature so each one is a deletable unit, and enforce that with Nx module-boundary lint rules so it's checked by CI rather than by convention. For runtime I lazy-load routes, use OnPush/signals, virtualise long lists, and set bundle budgets so a regression fails the build. And I keep state local by default — a global store everything writes to quietly re-couples the features you just isolated.”

question: Walk me through the architecture of your Angular application.
answer:
Answer in layers, and give a reason for each choice — the interviewer is testing whether you designed it or inherited it.

  • Structure — core/ (singletons: auth, interceptors, error handling), shared/ (dumb reusable UI), features/ (one lazy-loaded folder per domain).
  • Components — smart/container components fetch data; dumb/presentational ones take @Input and emit @Output, so they're trivially testable.
  • Services + DI — HTTP and business logic live in services, never components.
  • State — local first; a store only where state is genuinely shared.
  • Cross-cutting — interceptors for auth/errors, guards for routes.
tip: Every 'what' needs a 'why' with a trade-off. 'We used NgRx because it's standard' is a fail; 'we used NgRx because 4 features shared server state and we needed the devtools time-travel to debug it' is a pass.
code:
src/app/
  core/            # provided once: interceptors, guards, auth
  shared/          # dumb UI, pipes, directives — no feature imports
  features/
    orders/
      data-access/   # OrdersService, models
      feature-list/  # smart component (routed)
      ui/            # dumb components
  app.routes.ts    # lazy: loadChildren / loadComponent
deep:
How to structure the answer

Don't list files. Go top-down: what the app does → how code is organised → how data flows → cross-cutting concerns → trade-offs you'd revisit. Two minutes, then stop and let them drill.

1. Folder structure — and the rule behind it

LayerHoldsRule
core/auth, interceptors, global error handlingprovided once, app-wide
shared/dumb UI, pipes, directivesmust not import from features/
features/<domain>/everything for one domainlazy-loaded; no cross-feature imports

The invariant worth stating: dependencies point inward (features → shared → core), never sideways between features. If Orders imports from Billing, they should be one feature or share a library.

2. Modules or standalone?

Standalone components are the default from Angular 17+ — no NgModule boilerplate, and loadComponent lazy-loads a single route. If your app is NgModule-based, say so and say why (legacy, gradual migration) rather than pretending. Knowing both and having a migration opinion is the strong answer.

// standalone, lazy — the modern default
{ path: 'orders', loadComponent: () => import('./features/orders/list.component') }

// NgModule-based lazy route
{ path: 'orders', loadChildren: () => import('./orders/orders.module').then(m => m.OrdersModule) }

3. Smart vs dumb components

// Smart — knows about services, no template logic worth testing
@Component({ template: `<order-table [orders]="orders$ | async" (select)="open($event)" />` })
export class OrdersPage {
  private svc = inject(OrdersService);
  orders$ = this.svc.getOrders();
}

// Dumb — inputs/outputs only, OnPush, trivial to test and reuse
@Component({ changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush })
export class OrderTable {
  @Input() orders: Order[] | null = null;
  @Output() select = new EventEmitter<Order>();
}

Why it matters: dumb components have no dependencies to mock, so testing them is a function call. It's also what makes OnPush safe.

4. Where logic lives

  • Component — view state only.
  • Service — HTTP, business rules, orchestration. If a component has a HttpClient call in it, that's a smell.
  • Store — shared state across features.

Be ready for “why not put it in the component?” — because services are injectable, mockable, and reusable, and because a component's job is rendering.

5. State management — have an opinion

The honest senior answer is a ladder, not a religion: component state → a service with a BehaviorSubject/signal → a full store (NgRx/NgRx Signal Store) only when several features share server state and you need devtools/time-travel to reason about it. NgRx on a small app is boilerplate for its own sake — say that; it shows judgement.

6. Cross-cutting concerns

  • HTTP interceptors — attach the auth token, refresh on 401, map errors centrally.
  • Route guards — auth/role checks (CanActivate).
  • Global ErrorHandler — one place that logs to Sentry.

7. Close with a trade-off

Volunteer one thing you'd change: “we started NgModule-based and are migrating to standalone,” or “our shared/ folder became a junk drawer — I'd split it into ui/ and utils/.” Naming a weakness in your own architecture reads as seniority, not weakness — it proves you evaluated it rather than inherited it.

How to say it in an interview

“It's a lazy-loaded feature-based structure: core for app-wide singletons like interceptors and guards, shared for dumb reusable UI, and one folder per domain feature — dependencies point inward, features never import each other. Inside a feature I split smart components that inject services from dumb OnPush components that only take inputs, which keeps tests cheap. HTTP and business logic sit in services; state starts local and only gets promoted to a store when it's genuinely shared. Cross-cutting things — auth token, error mapping — are interceptors. The thing I'd revisit is X, because Y.”
